FT_IC1.evidence: Amsterdam Born Children and their Development (ABCD) study, Netherlands. Explicitly NOT the U.S. ABCD Study. Disqualifies paper per binding rule.

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time measured as TV-only and computer/video games. Per B5 and B7, TV-only and unspecified-platform gaming (without app/SM specificity) do NOT meet qualifying criteria. No qualifying smartphone or social media variables present.

FT_IC3.evidence: Does not meet FT-IC1; evaluation stops. Paper uses logistic regression with ORs (e.g., TV ≥1.5 h/day: OR 3.13, 95%CI 1.43-6.82) but population exclusion is binding disqualification.

FT_IC4.evidence: Paper fails on FT-IC1. While numeric values are extractable (ORs, CIs reported in results), the population criterion failure is binding.

FT_IC5.evidence: Population is Amsterdam ABCD (Dutch), not U.S. ABCD Study. Not eligible for inclusion.

FT_EC7_reason: FT-IC1 NOT_MET disqualifies paper. Not evaluated for FT-EC7.
notes: EXCLUDE. Wrong ABCD study (Amsterdam/Dutch, not U.S.). TV-only screen time does not meet qualifying exposure criteria. Binding population exclusion per FT-IC1 negative criteria.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study. N=4277 with usable structural imaging and screen media activity (SMA) data from first cross-sectional data release. Meets population criterion.

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen media activity (SMA) operationalized as: (1) Watch TV, (2) Watch videos/movies, (3) Play video games on computer/console/phone/tablet, (4) Test on cellphone, (5) Watch social media sites (Facebook, Twitter, Instagram, etc.). Per abstract and methods: 'SMA: e.g., watching television or videos, playing video games, or using social media.' Meets qualifying smartphone/SM variables per B1 binding rule (separate items for gaming and social media use reported). Seven potential answer choices included SMA items tracked independently.

FT_IC3.evidence: Group Factor Analysis (GFA) conducted with generalized linear mixed-effect models testing associations between SMA and brain structure (cortical thickness, sulcal depth, gray matter volume), internalizing/externalizing psychopathology, and cognition. Regression coefficients and effect sizes (Cohen's d ES: 0.04-0.1) reported. Meets inferential test criterion.

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric coefficients and effect sizes reported: Four SMA-related GFAs explained 37% of variance between SMA and structural brain indices. Effect sizes (Cohen's d ES 0.06-0.1 for externalizing, ES 0.08-0.1 for crystallized intelligence, ES 0.04-0.09 for fluid intelligence) reported in main text and abstract.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis from U.S. ABCD Study. Multivariate Group Factor Analysis examining SMA-brain structure associations and psychopathology/cognition outcomes. Primary data analysis, not review/protocol/measurement-only.

FT_IC6.evidence: No evidence of preprint/abstract/deposit duplicate. Published in NeuroImage 2019.

FT_EC7_reason: SMA is independent variable (predictor of brain structure/psychopathology/cognition), NOT dependent variable. Per binding rule, FT-EC7 requires SM to be DEPENDENT variable in EVERY analysis (condition a). Paper predicts outcomes from SMA, so FT-EC7 does not apply. Criterion (a) NOT satisfied.
notes: INCLUDE. U.S. ABCD Study with qualifying SM/smartphone variables (social media platform use + gaming + video watching). Multiple separate SMA items reported with own coefficients. Original empirical analysis with reportable effect sizes.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study. Explicitly mentions ABCD study examining effects on 'childhood and adolescent neurodevelopment and psychosocial and health outcomes.' Meets population criterion.

FT_IC2.evidence: Resource/technology review paper, not empirical measurement paper. Discusses mobile and wearable technologies and social media data but does NOT report any primary measurements or uses of qualifying SM/smartphone variables in original ABCD data analysis. Paper outlines 'current, future and potential use' of technologies; no extractable SM exposure variables from ABCD data analyzed here. Per FT-IC5 binding (B14), resource/data papers are excluded as NOT empirical analysis.

FT_IC3.evidence: No inferential tests reported. Paper is descriptive/prospective only, discussing future potential of mobile/wearable technologies and social media in ABCD study design. No regression, SEM, correlation, or hypothesis tests on SM ↔ outcome relationships.

FT_IC4.evidence: No numeric coefficients, effect sizes, or statistical results reported. Descriptive review article only.

FT_IC5.evidence: NOT original empirical analysis. Paper is a resource/perspective article (B14) describing current and potential incorporation of mobile/wearable technologies and social media methods into ABCD study infrastructure. No original data analysis. Pure methods/perspective paper.

FT_EC7_reason: FT-IC5 NOT_MET (resource/perspective paper, not empirical). FT-EC7 evaluation not applicable. Paper does not report any SM/demographic longitudinal analyses.
notes: EXCLUDE. Resource/perspective paper describing future potential of mobile/wearable technology and social media integration in ABCD study. NOT original empirical analysis. No SM exposure variables measured or analyzed. No inferential tests or numeric coefficients. Per binding rule B14, resource papers and perspectives fall under FT-IC5 NOT_MET.
